summary: This short post serves two purposes: announcing a Joel on Software reader dinner in Oslo, Norway, and highlighting the dangers of corporate relocations. Joel draws parallels between Blockbuster's move from Florida to Texas and Boeing's relocation of engineering jobs from California to Texas, noting how both moves resulted in massive loss of institutional knowledge when most employees refused to relocate.
key_points: Corporate relocations beyond a couple of miles cause significant employee attrition and loss of institutional knowledge
Blockbuster's relocation led to years of repeated mistakes as new employees lacked historical context of what had already been tried and failed
Boeing's relocation saw 80% of California engineers refuse to move, with investigators examining whether the resulting knowledge loss contributed to the Columbia disaster
money_quotes: When you relocate more than a couple of miles, some employees' lives would be too disrupted to make the move, so you lose a lot of employees, and all the institutional knowledge, skill, and experience that comes with those employees.
For years and years the business press watched agog as Blockbuster made mistake after inexcusable mistake, re-trying all kinds of ideas that had failed only two years earlier.
About 80% of the California engineers refused to relocate, forcing the company to hire workers in Texas and forfeit much of the experience of its California engineering base.
key_insight: Corporate relocations destroy institutional knowledge because most employees won't move, leaving the company to repeat past mistakes with an inexperienced workforce.
